notes: Only explicitly curated ratings are included.
Unrated records remain unrated and should not be guessed at in sort logic.

turkey-tail.basis: Widespread year-round hardwood bracket in Upper Midwest woods and deadfall.

chaga.basis: Restricted to birch and not encountered everywhere like turkey tail.

artists-conk.basis: Frequently encountered perennial hardwood conk across northern woods.

birch-polypore.basis: Regular where birch is present, but host-limited.

red-belted-conk.basis: A regular northern conk on conifer wood and mixed deadwood.

destroying-angel.basis: Important safety species but not an every-walk mushroom.

jack-o-lantern.basis: Regional but host- and site-limited compared with truly common wood fungi.

honey-mushrooms.basis: Fall clusters on roots, stumps, and trunks are regularly encountered.

dryad-saddle-pheasant-back.basis: Regular spring bracket on hardwood logs and trunks.

false-morel-gyromitra-group.basis: Important spring danger group but patchier and more habitat-tied than the common shelf fungi.

black-morels.basis: Sought-after spring morels that appear reliably in the right habitat, but not broadly common across all sites.

yellow-morels.basis: Regionally regular in productive habitat but not abundant across the general landscape.

half-free-morels.basis: Less broadly encountered than the main black/yellow morel buckets.

pearl-oyster.basis: Classic cool-weather oyster; regularly encountered on hardwood deadwood.

aspen-oyster.basis: Host-specific regional oyster rather than a broadly common generalist.

velvet-foot.basis: Regular enough in cold weather on hardwood, but not near the frequency of oysters or turkey tail.

chicken-of-the-woods.basis: Well-known, regularly encountered hardwood shelf in season.

hen-of-the-woods.basis: Beloved fall oak fungus but not broadly frequent across random sites.

lion-s-mane.basis: Distinctive and recurring, but still much less frequent than common brackets and oysters.

bears-head-tooth.basis: More specialized and less often encountered than lion's mane.

coral-tooth-fungus.basis: Localized and less often found than the better-known Hericium entries.

black-trumpet.basis: Can be abundant in good sites, but regionally patchy and easy to miss.

yellowfoot-chanterelle.basis: Reliable in the right northern conifer habitat but not broadly widespread across all habitats.

yellow-chanterelles-hardwood-complex.basis: Regular summer chanterelle type in suitable hardwood habitat.

yellow-chanterelles-conifer-complex.basis: More habitat-limited than the hardwood yellow group.

red-chanterelles.basis: Recognizable eastern chanterelle type but not one of the most broadly encountered regional mushrooms.

smooth-chanterelles.basis: Distinct chanterelle but not as routinely encountered as generic yellow chanterelles.

shaggy-mane.basis: Regular lawn and disturbed-ground mushroom in season.

meadow-mushroom.basis: Field agarics are regular but patchy and identification-sensitive.

hedgehog-mushrooms.basis: Regular late-summer to fall ground mushroom in suitable northern woods.

king-bolete.basis: A prized regional bolete, but not encountered as broadly as the everyday common mushrooms.

scaber-stalk-boletes.basis: A broad northern bolete group regularly found with birch, aspen, and other hosts.

bitter-bolete.basis: A frequent porcini lookalike in suitable woods.

blue-staining-red-pored-boletes.basis: Important caution group, but not a broadly ubiquitous field bucket.

old-man-of-the-woods.basis: Distinctive but not one of the region's most frequent boletes.

ash-tree-bolete.basis: Ash-tied bolete with narrower ecological placement.

bay-bolete.basis: Regionally regular but not a top-tier ubiquitous mushroom.

cauliflower-mushroom.basis: A recurring but habitat- and host-specific fall mushroom.

lobster-mushroom.basis: Good regional find in season but still patchy and dependent on host interactions.

shrimp-of-the-woods-aborted-entoloma.basis: Condition- and interaction-dependent specialty find rather than a routine encounter.

shrimp-russulas.basis: Known regional russula group, but not a truly common generalist bucket.

charcoal-burners.basis: Mild russula complex that appears regularly but not as one of the most widespread obvious finds.

short-stemmed-russula.basis: A broad, often-encountered white russula concept in season.

giant-puffball.basis: Conspicuous when present, but not broadly frequent across all habitats.

gem-studded-puffball.basis: A regular small puffball in woods and along paths.

pear-shaped-puffball.basis: A common clustered puffball on decaying wood.

witchs-butter.basis: Regular jelly fungus on dead twigs and branches in wet weather.

wood-ear.basis: Present but more substrate- and species-specific than common jellies and brackets.

elm-oyster.basis: Much less frequently encountered than true oysters and common shelves.

crown-tipped-coral.basis: Recurring on hardwood debris, but not broadly common.

beefsteak-fungus.basis: Showy but notably less common than mainstream hardwood polypores.

bicolor-bolete.basis: Not a routine Upper Michigan generalist compared with the more common bolete groups.

indigo-milk-cap.basis: Distinctive and regional, but not one of the most frequently encountered mushrooms.

saffron-milk-cap.basis: A pine-associated milkcap that can be regular in the right habitat.

orange-jelly.basis: Present on conifer wood but less broadly obvious than witch's butter.

parasol-mushroom.basis: Too patchy and habitat-specific to call common in the region.

shaggy-parasol.basis: Regular in some habitats but not a broadly common all-sites mushroom.

fairy-ring-mushroom.basis: Can be locally common in grass, but not a consistently widespread forest-foraging species.

rock-tripe-lichen.basis: Present in the region but substrate-limited and not a routine forager target.

reindeer-lichen.basis: Widely present in appropriate dry, open, or conifer-associated ground layers.

king-oyster.basis: Primarily cultivated/nonregional-wild context in this app.
